title: Best goal, best player, best fans - BBC reporters' World Cup awards
description: BBC Sport reporters pick their best moments of the tournament - and you can vote for yours.
fullText:
After delivering unforgettable drama and surprises across 104 matches over almost six weeks, the World Cup is over.

History has been scripted, records broken and heroes old and new made their mark at football's most coveted tournament.

Read our reporters' reflections from the US, Canada and Mexico and then choose from our chief football writer Phil McNulty's selections for best goal and best save.

Spain captain Rodri won the official World Cup Golden Ball trophy as the tournament's best player.

Messi won the Silver Ball as the second-best player, with Mbappe named Bronze Ball winner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Surprise, surprise. Lionel Messi. Not simply a swansong for a 39-year-old closing down his career, but the enduring inspiration of a nation and a football team. Look at how he controlled the game and pulled the strings when Argentina chased England down in a dramatic semi-final.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

I was in a Kylian Mbappe news conference early in the World Cup and I remember thinking that I'd never seen him looking more relaxed, more confident and more determined to make this his tournament. That demeanour off the pitch was reflected in some brilliant performances on the pitch - great goals, leadership, flair, composure under pressure and more work off the ball than I've ever seen from him.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The player of the tournament has been Jude Bellingham. The Real Madrid midfielder dragged England through by sheer force of will in some games and took his game to another level during the tournament.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Lionel Messi's leadership, football intelligence and ability to decide the biggest matches has proven vital for Argentina. He was the driving force behind their run to the final and that makes him the competition's standout player.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

With eight goals and four assists in eight games at this World Cup, it has to be Lionel Messi. England did their best to keep him quiet in the semi-final and succeeded in the first half, but Messi often finds a way and his two assists proved decisive.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Michael Olise. Marked out of the semi-final against Spain but the former Crystal Palace forward, who started his journey aged six at Hayes & Yeading United, has left his mark on this tournament with his seven assists. At just 24, Olise has another World Cup or two up his sleeve. I'm still playing replays in my mind of that spectacular scissor-kick against Sweden in the last 32. The whole press box erupted thinking it was going in, only for the ball to crash against a post.

Rodri. He has proved to be irreplaceable for his country as he is for Manchester City. Exceptional and so effective at the heart of the best team in the tournament

Easy choice but it has to be Lionel Messi. Doing this at the age of 39 is almost defying the law of physics. He is cementing his place as the greatest.

Spain's 19-year-old centre-back Pau Cubarsi received the Fifa award for the tournament's best young player after playing every minute of their World Cup campaign.

He was a key figure in the Spain defence that conceded only one goal all tournament.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

I am going for Spain's 19-year-old central defender Pau Cubarsi. The Barcelona youngster was magnificent against France in the semi-final and is a star of the future.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Lille fans, regular viewers of Ligue 1 and recruitment teams from the top clubs knew all about his potential but Morocco's Ayyoub Bouaddi burst on to the world stage in style with an exceptional performance in their opening match against Brazil. At the New York New Jersey Stadium that day, I found myself not watching the ball but watching his incredibly intelligent movement around the pitch, which reminded me of a cross between Patrick Vieira and Luka Modric.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

It sounds strange to call Erling Haaland a breakout star but it is hard to explain the impact he has had here in the US. He has become a household name and even more of a global superstar.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cape Verde's 40-year-old goalkeeper Vozinha. He began his unforgettable World Cup with seven sensational saves in a 0–0 draw against Spain in their debut World Cup match and ended it with eight more remarkable saves as Cape Verde pushed Lionel Messi and Argentina to the brink of elimination. Vozinha's heroics sparked a global following, with his Instagram audience skyrocketing from about 50,000 before the tournament to more than 29 million.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

It seemed like the USA would go far at this tournament, particularly with the wave of support behind them, but it wasn't to be as they exited at the last-16 stage. Despite that, many of their players did their own reputations no harm and one of those was full-back Alex Freeman, with his performances leading to USA boss Mauricio Pochettino saying he could be one of the best in the world in his position.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Step forward Switzerland's Johan Manzambi. Some 20-year-olds might be overwhelmed playing on the biggest stage of all. Not Manzambi. This huge talent plays with a smile on his face and is a natural entertainer who departed the tournament with three goals in four games.

Johan Manzambi. I highlighted him before the World Cup and, although he missed the end for Switzerland through injury, the fact he is moving to the Premier League with Aston Villa for a fee in the region of £50m underlines his quality.

The 17-year-old Mexico midfielder Gilberto Mora, who ended up in their starting line-up. Had the air of Luka Modric and seems destined for the top of the game.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The Azteca for Mexico v England. Might just be the best atmosphere I have ever experienced in seven World Cups. It was an assault on the senses and the singing of the Mexican national anthem was a spine-tingling experience. A simply unforgettable night.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Speaking to the Paraguay fans outside the Boston stadium, moments after one of the most dramatic penalty shootouts I have ever seen, as the South American side stunned Germany. One father and son, draped in Paraguay flags, were in tears as they told me that this was the best day of their lives, and it summed up what special moments at a World Cup can mean to people. I will never forget the emotion of those fans streaming out of the ground in disbelief.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Lionel Messi scoring a hat-trick in his first game at this World Cup showed that he was not done and was going to try to push Argentina to another tournament win.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Sidny Cabral's stunning 103rd-minute equaliser against Argentina. His strike from the edge of the penalty area that flew beyond Emiliano Martinez sent the stadium into disbelief and brought Cape Verde level at 2-2 in extra time. Although Argentina eventually edged the tie 3-2, Cabral's wonder goal became an instant classic and will always be talked about.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

For me it was Argentina being pushed by Cape Verde all the way before the debutants were knocked out. They showed the sort of guts and desire we want to see from these teams that gained the opportunity to play at a World Cup because of the expanded format.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

On my travels around the US, where I have covered 13 games in six different cities, I have spoken to dozens of fans. I was offered a beer and sausages cooked from the back of a pick-up truck before France-Paraguay in Philadelphia, and been stopped for selfies. "You're from the BBC? I have to have a selfie with the guy from the BBC." I have lost count of the number of times I have stopped a fan and introduced myself, only to be asked: "Is it coming home?"

For the moment I actually witnessed, it would have to be Sidny Cabral and his sensational equaliser against Argentina as Cape Verde, the smallest nation to ever reach the knockout stages, stunned the defending champions for a second time to make it 2-2 in Miami.

Cabral's astonishing goal against Argentina. It was as funny as it was breathtaking.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Brazil. Slow. Uninspired. Un-Brazilian. Trudged through the World Cup under Carlo Ancelotti and every weakness was exposed when they deservedly went out to Norway in the last 16.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Senegal came to the tournament with the most talented squad in their history but various problems off the pitch arguably meant the players were not given the right environment to thrive. When they were 2-0 up in the last-32 match against Belgium, it looked as though they would salvage something from this World Cup, but tactical errors and poor game management cost them. It was a massive wasted opportunity for a gifted generation of Senegal players.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Scotland missing out on third-placed qualification was a disappointment and having to wait a couple of days before it was confirmed must have been painful.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cristiano Ronaldo and Portugal. As one of the pre-tournament favourites, they fell short after a 1–0 last-16 defeat by Spain. Ronaldo managed three goals in five matches and, despite high expectations, Portugal never reached the level many expected, ending another World Cup campaign in frustrating fashion.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

The USA. They started this tournament so well and created a real wave of optimism and hope among the home fans, but all that was undone by the controversy surrounding Folarin Balogun and then their limp exit at the hands of Belgium.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

The final being held at the MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. Yes, it is huge but it is not fan-friendly and it is an expensive pain to get to and get away from. Philadelphia would have been my pick. A stadium on the outskirts of the city in a sports complex. The MetLife is a giant bowl in the middle of nowhere.

France in their semi-final defeat by Spain. I tipped Spain to beat France but still did not expect such a flat performance and their star-studded frontline to flounder as badly as they did.

I really thought Brazil under Ancelotti would go further. And clearly so did they.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Mexico. The sheer passion and love for their team and football was incredible. Also such friendly, welcoming fans and people. Incredibly warm and gracious in defeat.

We were in a restaurant in Mexico City the night before they played England, and an MC went around each table asking which country people were from. When we told them we were from England, it was announced to the rest of the diners, who burst into a spontaneous outbreak of applause.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

A word for the Curacao fans I was with inside the Philadelphia Stadium - because who knew that supporters from a nation of just 150,000 could make that much noise? Was there anyone left on the island? But the Brazil fans win it for me because they do not just take over stadiums; they take over cities; Philadelphia felt like Rio or Sao Paulo for 48 hours when they were in town.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The Colombia fans took over Arrowhead Stadium in Kansas City when they played Ghana, and made it feel like a party.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's fans were brilliant, with their legendary Viking row taking over the World Cup. The chant became a global sensation and created an unforgettable atmosphere in every stadium they entered. The most iconic moments came after matches when the players joined the fans to lead the Viking row from the pitch, uniting thousands of supporters in celebration.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

I was based mainly in Los Angeles for the World Cup. There's a large Mexican population there and from what I experienced, they were undoubtedly the best supporters. They were loud - very loud - for every Mexico game and when they won fireworks could be heard around LA while their distinct green jerseys were seen everywhere.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's Viking row was fun to watch, with fans wearing red shirts sitting in lines and rowing in rhythm. The Norway supporters also provided an amusing moment during the quarter-final with England. They sang "You're going home, you're going home, England's going home" as they led 1-0 in Miami. Moments later Jude Bellingham equalised.

The Mexicans. I was fortunate to experience two matches in the Azteca. That is where the heart of this World Cup was and the support for their team was a joy to behold.

Argentina. On two occasions in Miami and Kansas City, I have been in stadiums where their support has been in excess of 60,000 fans. The sheer scale of numbers is matched by their wild passion. They sing constantly and have generated the best atmospheres I have encountered in the US.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The deadlock was broken by Lionel Messi for Argentina against Cape Verde with a triumph of technique as he showed exquisite touch to take a difficult pass on the run before, with little room to work with, he guided it into the top corner with his second touch.

In the same game, Sidny Cabral's extra-time equaliser was a wonderful moment. From the angle just inside the area, his right-foot curling drive into the top corner gave Emiliano Martinez no chance.

In the group stages, Kerim Alajbegovic's goal for Bosnia-Herzegovina against Qatar was a superb solo effort - a weaving run before a rising right-foot shot of high class.

What looked like a casual swing of Erling Haaland's left boot was in fact a perfectly placed finish with astonishing power to crown a match-winning performance for Norway against Brazil in the last 16.

A tight quarter-final between Argentina and Switzerland was broken open by a moment of genius from Julian Alvarez. The vastly under-rated attacker provided with a swerving right-footed drive from long range into the top corner in extra time.

Antonio Nusa's opener for Norway against Ivory Coast was perfection. A little shimmy in the area to feint away from a defender, then a precise curling finish into the top corner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Johny Placide made a superb double save for Haiti against Morocco. It came first with his legs then he showed swift reactions to save one-handed to his left.

At the Azteca Stadium, Jordan Pickford had an outstanding game in England's memorable win over Mexico. His early save, diving low to his left to keep out Raul Jimenez's header, was a crucial moment of brilliance.

Orjan Nyland produced a remarkable stop for Norway against Brazil from his own defender Kristoffer Ajer, who inadvertently directed the ball goalwards. Nyland scrambled backwards showing remarkably anticipation and agility to leap and turn the ball against the post.

Aymen Dahmen showed stunning reactions for Tunisia against Japan from Takehiro Tomiyasu's close-range effort, somehow keeping the ball out as it did everything but cross the line.

There was a brilliant diving save by Patrick Beach for Australia in their group game with Turkey, diving away to his right to touch Abdulkerim Bardakci's rising 30-yard shot on to the post.

Iran's Alireza Beiranvand produced a remarkable save from Maxim de Cuyper of Belgium. Beiranvand could not reach Kevin de Bruyne's shot but somehow recovered his position and reacted to keep out a shot from inside the six-yard box.

Mbappe first to win Golden Boot twice as Rodri named best player

Lamine Yamal 'completes football' at 19 as Argentina betray Messi

World Cup is over, but the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Why Beckham was face of World Cup in the US
description: After arriving in Los Angeles 19 years ago promising to grow the game, David Beckham is still helping sell football to America.
fullText:
In America, nobody sells football quite like David Beckham.

During this World Cup, the former England captain has been impossible to miss.

On the pitch, the tournament belongs to stars like Kylian Mbappe, Jude Bellingham, Lamine Yamal and Lionel Messi.

Off the pitch, Sir David has become the face of the World Cup, fronting advertising campaigns for 11 brands ranging from McDonald's and Major League Soccer to Stella Artois, Home Depot and Bank of America.

And if you haven't seen him during one of the many half-time commercial breaks, you've probably spotted him at the United States' opening match in Los Angeles, watching alongside his friend Tom Cruise.

Just hours before that, Beckham had become the first professional footballer to receive a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame, with actor Cruise once again by his side.

More recently, he was seen singing Wonderwall and Hey Jude as he celebrated Jude Bellingham's two goals against Norway that sent England into the semi-finals.

"David speaks to so many different people. I think that's what's unique about him," said Stacy Jones, founder and CEO of the marketing agency Hollywood Branded.

"He brings in the female demographic because he is so sexy - but he appeals to men. He's an honest, good, authentic, down-to-earth type of individual. So you don't really get affronted by him."

She felt Beckham had achieved something few celebrities manage, moving effortlessly between football, fashion, entertainment, business and the cult of celebrity.

On Hermosa Beach, south of Los Angeles, hundreds of fans gathered to watch Spain reach the World Cup final at an event organised by LA Galaxy - the club Beckham joined in 2007 from Real Madrid.

Ask fans what comes to mind when they hear the name David Beckham and the answers come quickly.

"David Beckham was a really good player for England, but he's doing too many commercials now," said one football fan.

For Megan Hapeman, it's the recent McDonald's advert in which Beckham hopes to receive a cup featuring his own face.

Her six-year-old son Oliver proudly told me he still has one.

"When I got it, I was pretty proud," he said. "Because Mommy liked it, I liked it too."

That's exactly what brands are buying.

Beckham transcends clubs, generations and even football itself.

That broad appeal didn't happen by accident. It was built on Beckham's football legacy in America.

He remains the player with the trademark free-kicks, part of Manchester United's Treble-winning side in 1999, England captain and one of Real Madrid's original Galácticos.

Dan Courtemanche, Major League Soccer's executive vice-president and chief communications officer, admitted Beckham's influence on the game in America cannot be overstated.

"I always tell people he changed the fate of American soccer. Not once, but twice," he said.

When Beckham signed for LA Galaxy 19 years ago, stadiums across Major League Soccer sold out before he had even played a competitive match.

The league needed credibility and he gave it something even broader: mainstream celebrity.

Sixteen years later, Beckham helped persuade Argentina icon Messi to choose Inter Miami over rival offers globally - another moment that transformed MLS, according to Courtemanche.

"Lionel Messi decided to make Major League Soccer his league of choice and Inter Miami his club of choice," he added. "Again, it took us to a whole other level."

Tom Braun, LA Galaxy's president and chief operating officer, said Beckham's influence has stretched way beyond football.

"David's an iconic figure in our sport and our culture," he explained.

"He's built a business around his brand and continues to resonate with people."

The evolution of the 51-year-old, who also played for AC Milan and Paris St-Germain before retiring in 2013, was perhaps best symbolised this summer on Hollywood Boulevard.

When Beckham accepted his star on the Walk of Fame, he reflected he had never imagined "a working-class English footballer" would receive such an honour.

The recognition celebrated not only the life of Beckham the footballer, but Beckham the cultural figure.

"David Beckham, to America, is the World Cup," Jones said.

Having arrived in Los Angeles promising to grow the game, Beckham is still helping sell football to America.

Only now, he's doing it not with a free-kick, but with a brand.

When the World Cup finishes, the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Could Infantino really push through a 64-team World Cup?
description: If we have learned one thing from Gianni Infantino's Fifa tenure, it is that more is better. That could mean 64 teams at the World Cup.
fullText:
If we have learned one thing from 10 years of Gianni Infantino as Fifa president, it is that more is better.

More tournaments, more games, more money.

The new-look Club World Cup, the expanded World Cup, the inflated ticket prices.

And, of course, the Super Bowl-style show that meant the half-time break at the World Cup final lasted 27 minutes and 22 seconds.

Infantino appears to have thrown the door wide open to a 64-team World Cup.

"If you don't give smaller countries a chance to participate, they'll lack the incentive to keep improving," he said.

Except, of course, smaller countries are not denied the chance. If they are good enough, they qualify. See Cape Verde, Curacao, Jordan and Uzbekistan.

Purely in terms of the competition format, it does make sense.

The top two teams in the 16 groups would go through to the knockout stage. No longer would there be a need to send a few third-placed teams through.

But is there really any other logical reason to expand to 64 teams?

Let's start with how we got here.

The 2030 World Cup will mark 100 years since the first World Cup, which was held in Uruguay.

The tournament will primarily be hosted by Morocco, Portugal and Spain - but the opening three matches will be played in Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay as centenary hosts.

Conmebol - the South American confederation - wants more games.

In April 2025, it requested a one-off tournament of 64 teams so it could take on the full programme of the additional four groups.

Its president, Alejandro Dominguez, said it would ensure "nobody on the planet is left out of the party".

There has been little support from elsewhere.

Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin dismissed it as a "bad idea" for both the tournament and qualifying process.

Victor Montagliani - president of the governing body of North and Central America and the Caribbean (Concacaf) - said last year it would damage "the broader football ecosystem".

Asian Football Confederation (AFC) president Sheikh Salman bin Ibrahim Al Khalifa agreed, saying further expansion could bring "chaos".

None of which means it will not happen, with the decision taken by the 37 members of the Fifa Council.

A 64-team tournament has two main plus points: a more natural format, and more opportunities for emerging nations.

It is the latter which Infantino is championing, especially after the heroics of Cape Verde.

Fifa faced criticism before the tournament, with suggestions the additional teams would not be competitive.

Then Cape Verde drew with Spain and Uruguay to finish second in their group. And the Blue Sharks gave Argentina an almighty scare by taking them to extra time in the last 16, before losing 3-2.

But the fairytale stories must be balanced with the competitive edge of the tournament.

Haiti, Iraq, Jordan, Panama, Tunisia and Uzbekistan failed to pick up a point - and this summer's group stage had minimal jeopardy for the top nations.

Add more teams and it would be difficult to see a repeat of top-four seeds like Belgium (2022), Germany (2018), Spain (2014), Italy (2010) and France (2002) failing to make to knockout stage.

Then there is the question of where the extra places would go.

Based on this year's qualifying, these are the additional countries who would have made it into a 64-team World Cup 2026 (with their Fifa ranking at the start of the tournament in brackets):

AFC: Indonesia (118), Oman (79), United Arab Emirates (68).

Uefa: Denmark (21), Italy (12), Poland (36).

Concacaf: Honduras (65), Jamaica (71), Suriname (125).

Conmebol: Bolivia (77), Venezuela (49).

Africa: Burkina Faso (62), Cameroon (44), Gabon (86), Nigeria (26).

Six of those teams are from the top 50 of Fifa's world rankings - three from Europe, two from Africa and one from South America.

And therein lies the problem. Asia and Concacaf are weaker confederations, as was proven by performances this year.

Nine of Africa's 10 countries got out of the group stage. But only two of nine Asian countries advanced, and just three of the six Concacaf countries - the co-hosts - made it through.

New Zealand, who got one point and finished bottom of their group, are the only country from Oceania in the world's top 150.

There is an argument that if you extend again, you should give a greater weight of places to Europe just for competitive balance.

But, on the other hand, there are those who would argue that bringing more teams into the World Cup ultimately allows the game to develop further in those countries.

Infantino will sell the expansion as being good for the global game, but there have to be doubts that it is good for the competition.

Almost a third of Fifa's 211 nations would be present.

Would the group stage not simply become a glorified pan-confederation qualifying competition for what is, essentially, a knockout tournament?

Expanding the World Cup to 64 teams is really just a case of bolting on four additional four-team groups.

But more groups means more time.

This year's tournament lasted 39 days. An additional 24 matches would require 20 schedule slots. Or another five days.

But this summer's event was unique for two reasons: multiple time zones and indoor stadiums.

Fifa was able to elongate a matchday at either end, with games scheduled over a 14-hour period so it could stay true to exclusive time windows for television coverage.

That will not be so straightforward in the future, even with 48 teams.

Climate change is making playing in the daytime more challenging - see the record heatwaves in Europe in recent weeks.

Other countries are far less likely to have the covered venues of Atlanta, Dallas Houston and Vancouver to mitigate it.

The Saudi Arabia World Cup is likely to move to January 2035 but it is not clear how other countries will get around this growing problem.

Until this year, only once had there been dual hosts of the World Cup - Japan and South Korea in 2002.

It is hard to imagine any one country ever being capable of hosting again. Even Saudi Arabia.

Could we be looking at World Cups hosted by, for instance, the UK and Scandinavia? Or Eastern Europe?

How would an Asian or African country be able to fund hosting the World Cup?

Let's look a just a few issues that would need to be resolved.

This summer there were 16 host cities with stadiums having capacities ranging from 43,000 to 81,000. More would be required.

There would need to be 64 individual, elite-level training facilities for the qualifying nations.

Then there is the provision of hotels for the fans, and the infrastructure and support network to move them around.

Expanding the World Cup is not as simple as giving more opportunities to more countries.

One of Infantino's key promises when he was elected was to bring more money into the game.

Every football association now has access to $8m (£5.9m) in funding over a four-year period, with a further $1.2m (£890,000) on top for the smaller nations.

Confederations get $60m (£44.5m) to develop, promote and organise football.

It is why Infantino is likely to be re-elected unopposed next year.

This money has to come from somewhere, primarily the World Cup.

In 2017, Fifa projected a 48-team World Cup would bring in $6.5bn (£4.8bn).

In 2022, it revised the format again and added 24 games. Now, it expects to rake in $9bn (£6.7bn) this year.

A 64-team event is likely to raise more from television, sponsorship and ticket sales.

Fifa says it has a responsibility to grow the game, and its tool for this appears to be more and more football.

But critics would argue there has to be a point where expansion is no longer good for the game.

Why European backlash over Trump intervention won't worry Infantino

When the World Cup finishes, the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Factbox-Authorities restricting data centres amid AI boom
description: July 20 (Reuters) - A growing number of governments, regulators and cities around the world are moving to freeze, restrict or ban new data centre construction, as concerns mount over electricity costs
fullText:
July 20 (Reuters) - A growing number of governments, regulators and cities around the world are moving to freeze, restrict or ban new data centre construction, as concerns mount over electricity costs, strained ‌water supplies, land scarcity and the burden on local communities from the infrastructure powering the AI boom.

Governor Kathy Hochul imposed a one-year construction moratorium on data centres using 50 megawatts or more of ​power, making New York the first U.S. state to enact a full moratorium.

During the freeze, the state's Department of Environmental Conservation will not issue new discretionary permits while officials develop standards to assess the environmental impact of data centres.

Governor Janet Mills vetoed bipartisan legislation that would have enacted an 18-month moratorium on new data centres using more than 20 ‌MW of power.

Mills said she supported a ⁠moratorium in principle but objected to the bill's failure to carve out an exception for a specific project in the Town of Jay.

MONTEREY PARK, CALIFORNIA, U.S.

Residents voted to permanently ban ⁠data centres in Monterey Park in June 2026, becoming the first U.S. city to do so at the ballot box, following public backlash over a planned facility.

The Dutch capital imposed a one-year moratorium on new data centre developments in 2019. ​In April ​2025, it barred new data centres or expansions in the ​municipality until at least 2030.

Separately, the Dutch government's ‌2022 hyperscale ban restricts large facilities to two designated locations nationally, though Microsoft won approval in January 2026 for a project split into three towers, each individually below the size threshold.

DUBLIN, IRELAND (restriction now lifted)

Ireland's grid operator effectively blocked new data centre connections around Dublin from 2021 after warnings that the facilities were straining the grid.

The freeze ended in December 2025, with new connections now required to bring their own on-site power generation.

AUSTRALIA (planned legislation)

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese ‌said Australia would establish a government office to manage the development ​of AI standards, including rules for large data centres covering their location, ​power and water use, with legislation due early ​next year.

Australia does not currently have specific AI laws, relying instead on a range of ‌privacy and consumer protection laws and a voluntary ​AI ethics framework.

Denmark's government has ​introduced a draft bill to its parliament that would give new data centres the lowest priority for power grid connections amid growing capacity shortages.

The proposed law, backed by a broad cross-party agreement representing 80% of ​the parliament, would prioritise households, healthcare, ‌industry, transport and renewable energy projects instead.

Denmark's power grid has come under strain from a surge ​in electricity demand and connection requests, including from large data centres.

title: Fernandez sent off as Argentina lose World Cup final
description: Enzo Fernandez's 2026 World Cup ended in misery as the Chelsea midfielder was sent off in Argentina's final defeat to Spain.  The 25-year-old had already been booked for showing dissent towards referee Slavko Vincic when he made a poor challenge on Pau Cubarsi in the last minute of normal time.  Fernandez was subsequently sent off and received Argentina's first red card at a World Cup since 2006.
fullText:
Enzo Fernandez's 2026 World Cup ended in misery as the Chelsea midfielder was sent off in Argentina's final defeat to Spain.

The 25-year-old had already been booked for showing dissent towards referee Slavko Vincic when he made a poor challenge on Pau Cubarsi in the last minute of normal time.

Fernandez was subsequently sent off and received Argentina's first red card at a World Cup since 2006.

BBC co-commentator Alan Shearer said: "How daft is that from Enzo Fernandez?

"He is on a yellow card for dissent, not the tackle, but for dissent. He knows that, because that didn't happen long ago. For him to go into a challenge like that is crazy.

"He can protest as much as he wants, but it's the right decision. He gave the referee only one choice, and that was to show another yellow there for the red."

Fernandez had played a key role in helping his nation reach the final where they had the chance to defend their world title.

He will now have three weeks to rest and recover, as per Fifa guidelines, before returning to Stamford Bridge for the new 2026-27 Premier League season under new manager Xabi Alonso.

Chelsea kick off the new season at Fulham on Monday, 24 August.

title: Money, Geopolitics And Power Dominated The World Cup; Same Again In 2030?
description: The World Cup was widely considered to be the most controversial in history, but it simply continued a recent trend that looks set to continue in 2030 when the tournament heads to six countries
fullText:
When FIFA president Gianni Infantino handed over the governing body’s inaugural “Peace Prize” to Donald Trump last December, it seemed to epitomise collective concerns about what this summer’s tournament would become.

In some respects, we were wrong; after all, once the kick-off whistle blew and the soccer began, pre-match diatribe dissipated as people across the world focused on the winners, the scorers and the stars.

Pre-tournament unease about ICE agents attending games, expensive tickets and empty seats faded into the background, and even the U.S. president himself disappeared from view; he didn’t attend any matches, nor did he make any public statements.

That was until Trump’s intervention in the Balogun red card case sounded a wake-up alarm to remind us that this was the 2026 edition of FIFA’s showcase men’s competition, which was always going to be, arguably, the most controversial in history.

But many of us said that about Qatar 2022, where human rights, unsold tickets and a belief among some that the small Gulf nation was an undeserving host dominated the tournament discourse and narrative.

Given the turbulence, complexity and sensitivity of our world, perhaps the tournament is now established as a proxy for expressing global anxieties.

This poses some interesting questions, as does the six-nation hosting of the next men’s World Cup, which may be contested by sixty-four teams, rather than forty-eight at this summer’s edition (or the thirty-two that appeared in Qatar and previous World Cups).

From the U.S./Israel conflict with Iran, which led to immigration restrictions being placed on the latter’s national team, to a Somalian FIFA referee being denied entry to the U.S., it may seem difficult to imagine the 2030 tournament being anywhere near as controversial.

Yet the 2030 edition will take place across six countries: Argentina, Paraguay and Uruguay; and Morocco, Portugal and Spain, which will pose logistical challenges and create some troubling juxtapositions.

The 2026 final between Spain and Argentina was characterised as a “Palestine versus Israel” match; the current politics of Spain’s left-wing government standing in stark contrast to the right-wing politics of its counterpart in Buenos Aires.

And the early signs of a smooth 2030 tournament hosting are not promising; the South Americans have been accused of both brutal tactics on the field and racist abuse by fans off it.

Over in the West, trouble is already brewing; Morocco has been making bullish claims about hosting the 2030 final, even going so far as to build the world’s biggest football stadium, even though government officials in Madrid believe their city is the rightful destination for it.

Morocco has trouble along its borders, specifically with Algeria, with which it has a fractious relationship, though there are also ongoing disputes over Western Sahara and territorial claims to it.

In theory, Donald Trump will no longer be in office by 2030, though his influence may endure, not least in Morocco and Spain.

He has a close relationship with the King of Morocco; indeed, the U.S. administration has recently recognized Western Sahara as Moroccan territory, whilst the government in Rabat has cultivated increasingly strong relations with Israel.

Meantime, Trump has recently called Spain a “wasted cause” and a “terrible partner”, both for its perceived role in NATO and its vocal support for Palestine, issues that may ultimately undermine Madrid’s bid to stage the 2030 final, especially if Trump’s and Infantino’s bromance endures.

Adding a further dimension to Trump’s potential influence on the next tournament, his relationship with the right-wing conservative leaders of Argentina and Paraguay - Javier Milei and Santiago Peña respectively – means that we could see similar tactics being used to influence FIFA, sanction rivals, and sanitise the image and reputation of these countries.

Founded in 1904, FIFA’s original mission was to unify national football associations, standardize the laws of the game, and create a centralized international football championship.

When the first World Cup was staged in 1930, its purpose was to establish an independent, global football championship that allowed professional players to compete for a world title, bypassing the Olympic Games’ amateur-only restrictions.

However, since then, the World Cup appears to have morphed into something completely different, as this summer’s tournament became a convergence of big money, geopolitics and power.

The World Cup has been political in the past, for instance, in 1962, when Brazilian player Garrincha was sent off in the semi-final, prompting Chilean President Jorge Alessandri and Peruvian President Manuel Prado to strongly lobby FIFA on the player’s behalf.

Similarly, big money hasn’t been anathema to either the governing body or the tournament, with FIFA’s president, Joao Havelange, partnering in 1974 with Horst Dassler, the chief executive of Adidas, to transform the organisation commercially.

However, the 2026 edition has taken World Cup football into territory it hasn’t previously encountered, as the tournament became the embodiment of neoliberal ideology (dynamic ticket pricing illustrating this) and a geopolitical instrument (Trump’s Balogun intervention being a case in point).

The World Cup is no longer simply a football tournament; it has become a commercial behemoth, a Disneyfied entertainment product, and a nexus for countries and politicians seeking to accumulate and project power.

2030’s tournament will be no different.

In the past, when FIFA’s circus leaves town, most people seem to forget what all the fuss was about; after all, how many people spent this summer discussing migrant worker rights in Qatar (as many had done in 2022)?

But this summer’s tournament feels different; such was the profound nature of the issues often raised that, in 2030, one senses they will recur and intensify as our world becomes a more turbulent, complex and sensitive place.

This article was originally published on Forbes.com

title: Donald Trump's Sports ‘Curse' Trends Again After World Cup Final
description: “The jinx speaks.”
fullText:
Social media users joked on Sunday that Donald Trump’s infamous sportscurse had struck once again after Spain beat Argentina in the World Cup final.

The president, speaking to Fox Sports before the match, avoided making a concrete prediction on who would win.

But he did say Argentina captain Lionel Messi, whom he welcomed to the White House with his Inter Miami teammates in March, would be “hard to bet against.”

“I won’t pick sides, I just think it’s very hard to bet against Messi, he’s great,” said Trump.

Spain beat Argentina 1-0 after extra time thanks to Ferran Torres’ 105th-minute winner, securing the country’s second World Cup title after its first triumph in 2010.

The result promptedcritics to joke online that Trump had once again jinxed the team he appeared to back.

title: Neil Warnock delivers verdict on Tuchel’s use of Kobbie Mainoo
description: Manchester United midfielder Kobbie Mainoo endured a disappointing 2026 FIFA World Cup as England finished third. The youngster failed to play a single minute, spending his entire time across the Atla...
fullText:
Manchester United midfielder Kobbie Mainoo endured a disappointing 2026 FIFA World Cup as England finished third. The youngster failed to play a single minute, spending his entire time across the Atlantic on the bench.

Mainoo was expected to play a role for the Three Lions at the summer’s spectacle following an impressive second half of the campaign with the Red Devils. The 21 year old was initially overlooked by former head coach Ruben Amorim, struggling to find a place in his controversial 3-4-3 system.

However, as results failed to improve, the Portuguese lost his job in January, and the English giants appointed Michael Carrick to see out the season. The English manager immediately brought Mainoo back into the fold, and the youngster played a starring role in his team’s third-place finish.

He started all but one of United’s 17 games under Carrick, missing the defeat to Leeds United with an injury. Such was his impact that the Englishman, who was not part of Thomas Tuchel’s plans as early as January, secured a place in the World Cup squad. Unfortunately, things didn’t go according to plan at the tournament.

Mainoo’s ability to dominate possession and his new-found tenacity at United made him a secret weapon for Tuchel. Unfortunately, the German manager was not convinced by the player and did not afford him any minutes at the World Cup.

As such, the youngster watched from the bench as England reached the semi-final of the World Cup, where they were beaten by Argentina. Recent reports have suggested that Tuchel did try out Mainoo alongside Elliot Anderson in training ahead of the Ghana game amid concerns about Declan Rice’s fitness.

However, the German was not convinced and the United ace ended up watching from the sidelines. Liverpool legend Graeme Souness recently stated that Tuchel’s decision was the right one. However, former Crystal Palace manager Neil Warnock begged to differ.

Speaking on talkSPORT ahead of Saturday’s third-place play-off, Warnock insisted that Mainoo could have been a fine option off the bench for England. He said: “It will be interesting to see if he picks Mainoo. He was the type of lad that you needed in that last 25 minutes because he’s got the legs, he can hold it up, he can run with the ball.”

Warnock went on to slam the German manager’s treatment of the young midfielder, adding: “He’s commented about his training not being very good. You don’t say that to the press. You tell the player.”

“You put an arm around the player and say: ‘Look, you’re not training so good son, can you lift your percentage up?’ It’s totally unfair. Things like that should be kept inside the camp that it should be one-on-one with the player.”

“Will he play him now, and then he has a fantastic game and make Tuchel look stupid? Or does he just not bother playing in case he has a good game? I don’t think he’ll play him but listen he should do now. He should give everybody a game shouldn’t he?”

Mainoo missed England’s 6-4 win over France on Saturday with a knock. Tuchel confirmed after the game that the player suffered a back injury during training on Friday.

Mainoo’s experience at the 2026 World Cup may not have been a pleasant one, but it will help shape his career. The Englishman will now have a point to prove next season and United fans will hope that he can continue his recent good run with the club.

Featured image by Molly Darlington/Getty Images

title: First performance tests at Säbener Straße
description: FC Bayern kick off their pre-season preparations for the 2026/27 season under the guidance of head coach Vincent Kompany today. Whilst the World Cup participants will be phased back into training ove...
fullText:
FC Bayern kick off their pre-season preparations for the 2026/27 season under the guidance of head coach Vincent Kompany today. Whilst the World Cup participants will be phased back into training over the coming weeks, Sven Ulreich, Tom Bischof, João Palhinha, Bryan Zaragoza, Sacha Boey and Arijon Ibrahimović from the first-team squad are already available. Serge Gnabry, Lennart Karl, David Santos Daiber and Cassiano Kiala will continue to be gradually reintegrated into the squad as part of their rehab.

First up are performance diagnostics. The majority of the World Cup participants including the Germany internationals will undergo the relevant tests on 27 and 28 July and then travel individually to the training camp at the Tegernsee. Luis Díaz, Konrad Laimer and Josip Stanišić will join the squad ahead of the Audi Summer Tour to Jeju and Hong Kong (departure on 1 August). Harry Kane, Michael Olise and Dayot Upamecano will join the squad upon their return from Asia, as will Jamal Musiala, Alphonso Davies, Ismael Saibari, Gnabry and Karl, who will train in Munich.

During the training camp at the Tegernsee from 27 to 30 July, there will be daily open training session at 15:30 CEST. The friendly against FC Rottach-Egern on 30 July will, as always, be available to watch via a free live stream on FC Bayern’s official channels.

title: Real Madrid new signing Cucurella gets emotional after Spain win World Cup: ‘It’s not easy to get here’
description: Real Madrid new signing Marc Cucurella was one of Spain’s standout performers during the 2026 FIFA World Cup, playing a key role in the team’s triumph with consistently impressive displays at left...
fullText:
Real Madrid new signing Marc Cucurella was one of Spain’s standout performers during the 2026 FIFA World Cup, playing a key role in the team’s triumph with consistently impressive displays at left-back.

Following Spain’s victory over Argentina in the final, during which he excelled once again, attention quickly turned to a promise the defender had made before the tournament: if La Roja won the World Cup, he would get a tattoo of head coach Luis de la Fuente.

Speaking to the media after the celebrations in New Jersey, Cucurella confirmed that he intends to keep his promise.

“I don’t know, I have to think about where to get it. People will ask me, it has to be somewhere visible. We have a long trip ahead and they’ll give me ideas. I’ll have to do it,” Cucurella said with a laugh, as quoted by Mundo Deportivo.

The new Real Madrid defender defender laughed as he admitted there was no way out of the bet now that Spain had become world champions.

Cucurella was key to Spain’s World Cup triumph. (Photo by Carl Recine/Getty Images)

Reflecting on the achievement, Cucurella became emotional as he spoke about the challenges he faced throughout his career.

“It’s not easy to get here. It’s tough during your teenage years; you have to make decisions. Sometimes there are people who tell you you’re not good enough and that you won’t make it,” Cucurella said, almost tearing up.

“To all those people, I’d like to thank them for taking the time to give me their advice. Also to everyone who has helped me – we’ve both been stubborn. Today we’ve been rewarded for being here. It hasn’t been an easy summer for us, so we’re really happy.”

Talking about the team’s performance Cucurella felt Spain deserved to lift the trophy after controlling the final against the defending champions.

“We already knew what to expect. They tried to play their own style of football, with a lot of intensity. The team was fully focused, didn’t get drawn into a mind game, and we deserved the victory,” said Cucurella.

“We were playing against the reigning champions and we dominated the match,” he emphasised.

The left-back also praised Spain’s evolution throughout the tournament.

“It’s incredible. From the very first day we arrived, we tried to play our own style. We started off a bit slowly, but we kept improving and ended up playing some incredible football. We dominated France and Argentina and didn’t concede a single goal.”

Spain finished the tournament having conceded just one goal, setting a new defensive benchmark for a World Cup-winning side.

World champion. (Photo by Justin Setterfield/Getty Images)

For Cucurella, that achievement reflected the collective effort of the entire squad, as he said:

“When you don’t concede, it’s much easier to win. We’ve all been doing an incredible job. Take the way Oyarzabal pressed, for example. It’s a team effort. We attack and defend together. That’s the key.”

Cucurella then acknowledged that they were still coming to terms with being champions

“We haven’t had much time. We’re looking forward to getting back to Spain, where there are lots of people who haven’t been able to come, and we’re keen to celebrate with all our fans. We’d like to thank them for their support; we’ve felt right at home.”

Before leaving for the airport, Cucurella also praised Rodri, who has been constantly linked with Real Madrid, and won the Golden Ball at the World Cup.

“He’s very important; sometimes it’s not about scoring or providing assists. He controls everything, organises the team – when we press, when we attack… he’s very important,” Cucurella said.

“Whenever we’re under pressure, he gives us the chance to play through him. He’s had an incredible tournament and deserves the award.”

title: Stay or go - at 28, Rashford faces big Man Utd call
description: The World Cup is over, and Marcus Rashford returns home not knowing if his future at club level lies with Manchester United.
fullText:
Marcus Rashford's World Cup ended with a considered and thoughtful social media post, issued in the aftermath of England's amazing 6-4 third-place play-off victory over France in Miami.

Disappointments are guaranteed in football, he said, before opening out in a more positive vein and stating firmly: "Keep believing in us. Our time will come."

He added: "Now it's time for me to rest, reflect, spend some quality time with family and friends, and come back a better version of myself."

'Reflect' is the big word in that overview.

At 28, Rashford is moving into what should be the prime years of his career.

He has just won his first league title, La Liga with Barcelona, has 77 England caps and featured in six out of eight games as Thomas Tuchel's side delivered the country's best World Cup performance since 1966.

But in reality, a large part of what Rashford will be reflecting on with his family - some of whom are also key figures in terms of guiding his career - is whether his club future lies at Manchester United.

Rashford is contracted to Manchester United until 2028. His salary of £325,000 a week makes him the club's top earner at a time when minority owner Sir Jim Ratcliffe is trying to stay on top of salary costs, which are, by some distance, the club's highest expenditure.

Barcelona had an option, in the loan deal they agreed for Rashford last season, to turn it into a permanent transfer for £26m.

As expected, that deadline passed in the middle of June without it being activated.

There was another clause in Rashford's contract that allowed other clubs - Liverpool and Manchester City excepted - to buy him for £40m, which expired earlier in July. As expected, no-one triggered that either.

Sources close to the player felt it was illogical to think a club would come in and offer more than Barcelona would have had to pay.

With England's World Cup campaign at an end, Rashford is now entitled to a three-week break.

Unless the timings are altered, or his situation changes, he will return to Manchester United just as they begin a six-day training camp in the Republic of Ireland, scheduled around an historic meeting with old rivals Leeds at Croke Park on 12 August.

Suggestions around Rashford have been that he is relaxed about the prospect of returning to training at United.

For a start, there is not the same confrontational relationship between the player and his coach.

Ruben Amorim fell out with the forward within weeks of his arrival from Sporting Lisbon. Amorim eventually exiled him, which triggered a loan move to Aston Villa.

Then the head coach put Rashford in his 'bomb squad', along with Alejandro Garnacho, Antony, Tyrell Malacia and Jadon Sancho, when pre-season training began last summer.

The other four players have all now left the club.

Amorim's replacement, Michael Carrick, is a former team-mate of Rashford's. He has also been his coach and was, briefly, his manager, following Ole Gunnar Solskjaer's dismissal in 2021. The pair are said to get on well.

Carrick's only public statement around Rashford, towards the end of last season, was conciliatory in nature, which is in keeping with his general personality.

He is so far yet to be asked what his stance is around the player returning to the fold.

For their part, United are sticking to their refusal to renegotiate a deal with Barcelona on reduced terms or consider a second loan to the Catalan giants.

It is not clear what interest there is in Rashford at the Nou Camp given Barca have already signed England wide-man Anthony Gordon and are about to bring in Germany forward Karim Adeyemi from Borussia Dortmund.

The consistency of United's messaging is being articulated in a summer where they are keen to prove they will not be bullied into overpaying to get deals over the line.

That financial discipline may have consequences - while the feeling was Elliot Anderson would have joined Manchester City anyway, United did not countenance paying the £116m Nottingham Forest eventually sold him for.

The same is true of Mateus Fernandes and his £85m switch from West Ham to Tottenham.

Nevertheless, Manchester United's enhanced used of data through director Michael Sansoni and his dedicated team means the club feel bullish about their chances of landing alternative targets who offer better value.

Clearly just as United need active sellers to buy from, they need buyers so they can offload those players they do not want.

In Rashford's case, there are a limited number of clubs globally who could match his current salary. And where a club are interested, the player has to want to join them.

Judging by his recent social media post, he would not do anything to jeopardise his England place.

BBC Sport has also been told of some Premier League clubs he would not be keen to move to.

So where will Rashford be when the transfer window closes on 1 September? Currently, the likeliest answer, by some distance, is Manchester United.

After that: Any Spanish club, any Turkish club, any German club. The vagueness there underlines the current lack of market for the 15th highest scorer in United's history, with 138 goals - someone who is only 17 short of a place in the top 10.

There are plenty, including former captain Gary Neville, who feel Rashford's time at Old Trafford is over and there should be no reconciliation.

In the end though, it may have to happen - and not everyone would be disappointed.

"A fit and focused Marcus Rashford has the ability to be one of the best players," said Rashford's friend Sam Smith - the Wrexham forward who scored the winner in their pre-season victory over Carrick's team in Helsinki on Saturday - when speaking to the United We Stand podcast.

"He has had a difficult time at United and getting away from it all was a good thing.

"I don't know what is going to happen but as his friend, I hope he comes back and proves how good a player he was, how good he can be and starts scoring goals again."

title: Who am I? Guess World Cup star No 43
description: Will you get today's player in as few attempts as possible?
fullText:
The rules are simple. Each day there's a new footballer and the challenge is to guess who they are in as few attempts as possible.

After each wrong guess you unlock a new clue. Guess the answer after as few clues as possible to score more points.

Three is a good score, four or five points is exceptional.

title: Drubbed in Durban, but Morgan says Wales are closing gap to elite
description: Jac Morgan is confident Wales are closing the gap to the best in the world despite another hammering by South Africa in the Nations Championship.
fullText:
Jac Morgan believes Wales are closing the gap to the best sides in the world despite chastening Nations Championship defeats by Argentina and South Africa.

Steve Tandy's side enjoyed a 39-24 victory over Fiji in the opening round in Cardiff but then suffered comprehensive losses to the Pumas and Springboks.

The world champions rotated their side for the round three fixture, giving debuts to four players, but still eased to a seven-try 43-0 win.

The Springboks would have challenged their 73-0 November win in Cardiff were it not for errors in the 22, but Morgan insists Wales are closing the gap to the big hitters.

"I think we are – or at least we hope we are," said the flanker.

"You can see the progress we've made since the autumn."

Mounting questions for Wales after Durban defeat

Tandy bitterly disappointed by South Africa drubbing

Wales finished a first season under Tandy with a record of three wins, against Japan, Italy and Fiji, from 12 Tests.

They are 11th in the world rankings - bizarrely moving up a place courtesy of the Azzurri's heavy loss in Australia - as they count down to next year's World Cup, when they face England, Tonga and Zimbabwe in the group stages.

"The squad has developed, particularly with our performances against Scotland and Ireland during the Six Nations, and then the win over Italy," said Morgan.

"We were pleased with the victory over Fiji at the start of the summer as well because we know how difficult they are to beat.

"We just have to keep developing and we've been doing that as a squad. There are still plenty of lessons to learn, but the more we play together, the more experience we gain and the more we can improve."

Morgan, who is now officially a Gloucester player after his summer move from Ospreys, missed eight Tests after suffering a shoulder injury against Argentina in Tandy's first game.

The British and Irish Lions flanker says that Wales are leaving no stone unturned in their bid to close the gap as quickly as possible.

"We're a very close group and everyone wants to get better," said Morgan, who was captain for Tandy's first game.

"I'm proud of the effort they've put in, not just over these last three weeks but throughout the whole season.

"It's clear that everyone wants to learn, improve and continue developing together as a squad."

Wales play four Nations Championship fixtures in November against Japan, New Zealand and Australia in Cardiff before facing another southern hemisphere opponent at finals weekend in Twickenham.

Spain Hero Ferran Torres Career, Girlfriend, & Family: 5 Fast Facts You Need to Know
description: Ferran Torres became Spain's World Cup hero by scoring the only goal in a 1-0 victory over Argentina in the 2026 final. The post Spain Hero Ferran Torres Career, Girlfriend, & Family: 5 Fast Facts You Need to Know appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
Ferran Torres became Spain’s World Cup hero after scoring the only goal in a 1-0 victory over Argentina in the 2026 FIFA World Cup final. The Barcelona forward broke the scoreless tie in the 106th minute of extra time at MetLife Stadium, delivering Spain its second World Cup championship. The strike was Ferran’s first goal of the soccer tournament and the third World Cup goal of his international career. Here’s what you need to know.

Spain and Argentina remained scoreless through the first 90 minutes and the opening period of extra time before Ferran finally broke through.

The forward scored in the 106th minute, giving Spain the 1-0 advantage it protected through the final whistle. His goal secured the 2026 World Cup title and became the biggest moment of his international career.

Ferran entered the final without a goal in the tournament, but he delivered when Spain needed him most. The winner raised his career total to three World Cup goals and added another major trophy to a résumé that already included Spain’s Euro 2024 championship.

Ferran was born in Foios, Valencia, on February 29, 2000. Because he was born on Leap Day, his birthday only appears on the calendar once every four years.

A lifelong Valencia supporter, Ferran joined the club’s youth academy when he was 6 years old. He made his senior debut against Eibar on December 16, 2017, becoming the first player born in 2000 to appear in La Liga.

Ferran moved to Manchester City in 2020 and won the Premier League and EFL Cup during the 2020-21 season. Barcelona signed him in January 2022, bringing him back to Spain.

Despite struggling at times during his first two seasons, Ferran helped Barcelona win a domestic treble in 2024-25 before enjoying the best scoring campaign of his career the following season with 21 goals as the club captured another La Liga title.

IT'S FERRAN TORRES IN THE FIFA WORLD CUP FINAL 🇪🇸

THE ENTIRE SPAIN BENCH GOES TO CELEBRATE WITH HIM! pic.twitter.com/RVRXvnyOXG

— FOX Sports (@FOXSports) July 19, 2026

Ferran normally plays as a striker for Barcelona, but he has also been effective on both wings.

Spain used him on the right side during parts of the World Cup while Lamine Yamal recovered from injury. His versatility gave Spain another dangerous attacking option throughout the tournament.

Ferran entered the World Cup in outstanding form after finishing as Barcelona’s joint-leading scorer in La Liga with 16 goals. He also scored against Iraq and assisted against Peru during Spain’s final warm-up matches before the tournament.

His ability to play anywhere across the front line has helped him total 84 goals and 17 assists in 358 senior appearances for club and country.

Ferran was raised by his mother, M. Dolores Garcia Alcover, and grew up alongside his older sister, Arantxa.

Their relationship remains one of the closest in his life. While playing for Manchester City, the siblings even decided to get matching tattoos to celebrate their bond.

“We’ve always been so close, always together, in the good moments and in the bad ones, and this is why we decided to both have the same tattoo, because we are very important to each other,” Ferran said.

Ferran has kept much of his family life private and has rarely spoken publicly about his father.

Lamine Yamal, Ferran Torres, Dani Olmo, Pedri González, Pau Cubarsí, Eric Garcia, Joan Garcia… WORLD CHAMPIONS!

📸 @fifaworldcup pic.twitter.com/Nt6tktf2WA

— FC Barcelona (@FCBarcelona) July 19, 2026

Between 2021 and 2023, Ferran was in a relationship with professional equestrian Sira Martínez, the daughter of former Spain manager Luis Enrique, according to Yahoo Sports.

Sira competes in show jumping and is a member of the Barcelona Royal Polo Club. The couple began dating while Ferran played for Manchester City and stayed together after his transfer to Barcelona.

According to multiple reports, they ended their relationship in 2023 as Sira focused on her equestrian career. Based on Ferran’s public social media activity, he is currently believed to be single.

Away from soccer, Ferran has often cited former Spain striker David Villa as his childhood idol, while former Valencia star Gaizka Mendieta was the name on his first Valencia jersey.

title: Donald Trump wants World Cup back in U.S. 'immediately'
description: Donald Trump wants the World Cup in the U.S. again while still in office, but he will have to wait awhile for a return.
fullText:
The United States just finished hosting the 2026 World Cup for the first time in more than 30 years, and even though it will be a while before it does it again, President Donald Trump will be requesting to host it once more "immediately."

Trump made the comments to Fox Sports' Jenny Taft ahead of the World Cup final between Spain and Argentina on Sunday, July 19, lauding the success of the tournament that saw millions of people flock to the U.S., Canada and Mexico, and take part in a widely successful event.

He admitted to FIFA president Gianni Infantino that he was skeptical of how well the World Cup would do in the U.S., but the great turnout and reception resulted in him wanting it once more.

"I had a great idea for Gianni. I said, ‘You have to do two countries.’ So announce us again next time, and announce another country after that, that’ll take out some of the anger and the shock," Trump said. "But based on the numbers, we will be requesting it again immediately. But I said to take out the shock, you could announce us and maybe then for the next one another, and even another. But we have to do this again, and we have to do it while I’m around. You hear that, Gianni?”

If Trump was referring to the World Cup being in the U.S. again while he is in office, it won't happen since his term ends in 2028. The 2030 World Cup will be hosted by Morocco, Spain and Portugal, while the 2034 edition will be in Saudi Arabia.

The next available World Cup is in 2038, which the U.S. could very likely host since Oceania and North America will be eligible to host under the current cycle rules. However, that's in 12 years.

Trump has made his presence within FIFA known since 2018, when the U.S. was awarded the hosting duties for the 2026 tournament. He has become a close friend with Infantino, who presented Trump with the FIFA Peace Prize in December in a controversial move. He also inserted himself into the red card conversation surrounding U.S. star Folarin Balogun, when he pushed Infantino to reverse the decision to suspend him for one match, which ended up happening in another decision surrounded by controversy.

The president was inside MetLife Stadium for the final between Spain and Argentina, and was on the field to present the medals and trophy for Spain's title, which included him awkwardly still on stage as La Roja celebrated holding the trophy.

title: From Super Bowl to World Cup: The major sporting events Trump has attended in his 2nd term
description: President Donald Trump has prioritized attending huge sporting events during his second term.
fullText:
President Donald Trump has prioritized attending huge sporting events during his second term.

On Sunday, Trump will become the first U.S. president to attend a men’s World Cup final when Argentina and Spain face off in one of the year’s most anticipated sporting events. His appearance marks just the latest sporting event he will attend since returning to office in January 2025.

Here’s the list of other major sporting events he has attended since returning to office.

Trump became the first sitting U.S. president to attend a Super Bowl during on Feb. 9, 2025 in New Orleans. The game came about a month after a New Year’s Day terrorist attack in the city killed 14 people and injured dozens more. Trump met with families of the victims before the game.

Trump left the game about 10 minutes into the second half—which left pundits speculating that he left early because his desired team, the Kansas City Chiefs, was losing.

Trump, who is close with UFC President Dana White, has attended multiple fights during his second term in office. He was met with cheers when he attended a fight at the Kaseya Center in Miami alongside Secretary of State Marco Rubio in April 2025. He later attended a UFC match with Rubio at the Prudential Center in Newark, New Jersey in June 2025.

Trump reaffirmed his love for the UFC after he recently hosted a fight on the South Lawn of the White House on June 14 to celebrate America’s 250th anniversary.

Trump attended the 2025 Daytona 500 just weeks after going to Super Bowl LIX. He had also attended the popular race in 2020 during his first term in office. His appearance was marked by his motorcade taking a lap around the Daytona International Speedway.

Trump’s appearance at the World Cup final on Sunday is not the first time he attended a FIFA event. Last year, Trump was mocked after he awkwardly joined in on Chelsea FC’s FIFA Club World Cup celebration.

Chelsea winger Cole Palmer later said he did not realize Trump would be on the stage as his team lifted up their trophy.

“I knew he was going to be there, but I didn’t know he was going [to] be on the stand where we lift the trophy. So, I was a bit confused,” said Palmer, via USA TODAY.

Trump faced widespread criticism after his attendance at the U.S. Open men’s tennis final snarled security lines, which delayed fans getting into the USTA Billie Jean King National Tennis Center in September 2025.

The 2025 match between world No. 1 Jannik Sinner and No. 2 Carlos Alcaraz started about 30 minutes late as a result of enhanced security measures. Trump also faced backlash after it was reported that US Open officials sent an email to broadcasters asking them to censor any negative reactions to his presence.

Trump made waves after fans blamed the president for “cursing” the New York Knicks with his attendance at Game 3 of the NBA Finals at Madison Square Garden.

The NYPD and the Secret Service cancelled the popular watch party outside the world’s most famous arena, leaving die-hard fans outraged. Fans were also prohibited from bringing bags inside the arena, and were told to expect enhanced security measures before entering the game.

Trump was also met with boos as he was shown on screen before tip-off.

The Knicks ended up breaking their 13-game winning streak in Game 3, making it their only loss in the best-of-seven matchup with the San Antonio Spurs.

title: Leandro Paredes sent off after final whistle as Argentina’s World Cup ends with punches and in disgrace
description: Paredes was involved in an ugly clash with Spain’s Eric Garcia and Gavi following the full-time whistle
fullText:
Argentina’s World Cup ended with punches thrown and the former champions left in disgrace following a 1-0 defeat to Spain in the final.

There were chaotic scenes after the full-time whistle in New Jersey as the Spain substitutes ran onto the pitch to celebrate victory.

Nahuel Molina, the Argentina defender, appeared to swing a punch into the stomach of Spain captain Rodri as he ran past him onto the field.

Then, there was a violent clash involving midfielder Leandro Paredes, who was pictured shoving Spain’s Eric Garcia by the throat.

Paredes wrestled Spain’s Gavi to the ground and pushed into his face while holding him by his substitute bib.

Spain’s Mikel Oyarzabal and Rodri sprinted past Molina after the final whistle was blown and La Roja were crowned champions.

Molina appeared to swing at Rodri, leading to the Spain captain stopping in his tracks and approaching the Argentina defender while shouting.

Molina then squared up to Rodri and bumped him in the chest, which was the flashpoint that triggered Spain’s Eric Garcia to step in.

Paredes, who was booked during the final and was fortunate to not receive a second yellow card, then flew towards Garcia. He jabbed him in the throat just as Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni approached, wagging his finger and trying to break up the scuffles.

With Scaloni separating Garcia and other Argentina coaching staff members getting involved, Paredes then turned his attention to Spain’s 21-year-old midfielder Gavi - who by that point was already wrestling with Argentina’s Thiago Almada.

Paredes, 32, spun Gavi to the ground and appeared to push into his face while holding him by the bib. Several of Gavi’s Spanish team-mates then stepped in to shield him, with Spain’s Borja Iglesias crashing into Paredes.

It was a sorry way for Argentina’s tournament to end after they had taken an emotional path to the final. They were attempting to a second World Cup in a row for captain Lionel Messi, who was playing on the World Cup stage for the final time.

But the antics of the rest of the Argentina team were quickly condemned as the scenes after the final whistle soured Spain’s deserved victory, with former England goalkeeper Joe Hart labelling Argentina’s behaviour “disgusting”.

"It's a disgrace,” Gary Neville said on ITV. “I love the competitiveness of the Argentines, fighting and scrapping for their lives, Messi has propped them up, but it’s been a disgrace the last few games.”

“Paredes threw two or three haymakers into someone's face. He went for them,” Alan Shearer said on BBC. “There is no place or space for that.

“We know how much it means to them and we know how much it hurts to lose, but there is a way to lose. Too many times we have seen that from Argentina. The reaction after the final whistle is terrible.”

BBC pundit Hart said: “There was one man on the pitch who did have class and that was Lionel Messi, who shook hands with every one of those Spanish players.“The game’s done. They could have absolutely no complaints about what happened today. Disgusting behaviour.”

Hart added: “Spain fully deserve it. They have been absolutely superb all tournament.“They have dominated all game against a rigid, rugged Argentina. The best team has won this World Cup.”

Argentina were previously criticised for their aggressive behaviour during their 2-1 semi-final win over England, after which players unveiled a banner claiming sovereignty of the Falklands Islands.

Fellow pundit Wayne Rooney said of Argentina’s behaviour: “Does it surprise you? It doesn’t surprise me.“We’ve seen this with Argentina before. It’s just not the reaction you want. We’ve seen some of the reaction after knocking England out in the last round.“That’s just sad. If you lose a game of football, be graceful and get out of there. It’s really poor from them.”

ITV’s co-commentators were in agreement that Spain were deserved winners, with Lee Dixon accusing Argentina of “anti-football” and questioning how Paredes had remained on the pitch, while Ally McCoist said Spain were “the only team trying to win this game”.

Meanwhile, the tears rolled down Lionel Messi’s face as he watched the Argentina fans following defeat to Spain.

Messi, 39, is yet to confirm his future plans but the expectation is that one of the greatest to grace the game has played his final World Cup before retirement.

The Argentina captain was left frustrated as Spain dominated the final, with the European champions winning 1-0 thanks to Ferran Torres’ goal after extra time.

Messi had inspired a series of brilliant comebacks in the knockout stages to help Argentina to a second successive final but Spain were deserved winners.

Messi scored eight goals in an incredible tournament and the Argentina fans chanted his name as Spain’s Rodri collected the player of tournament award.

Messi then led his team-mates to collect their runners-up medals. He then stood and cried while he watched the Argentina fans serenade him.

The record eight-time Ballon d’Or winner will be 43 at the next men’s World Cup, which takes place in Spain, Portugal and Morocco - but will kick off in Argentina.

Messi waited until Spain lifted the World Cup, but then quickly left the pitch in what was perhaps a bitter end to his World Cup journey.

Elsewhere, Argentina centre-back Cristian Romero snubbed Donald Trump’s handshake during the presentation of the runners-up medals.

Romero started the match at the heart of the Argentine defence, alongside Lisandro Martinez, but was subbed off at the 70-minute mark.

However, the 28-year-old Tottenham defender still made his mark after that as he snubbed a handshake from Trump when the US president was conducting the medal presentation.

Trump was handing out medals to both teams, as well the individual tournament awards, and Romero shook the hand of both Fifa president Gianni Infantino and Mexican president Claudia Sheinbaum, who were either side of Trump, but did not engage with the US president.

title: Is Trump the first president to attend a World Cup final?
description: President Donald Trump will be attending the final men’s World Cup game in East Rutherford, New Jersey on Sunday afternoon.
fullText:
President Donald Trump will be attending the final men’s World Cup game in East Rutherford, New Jersey on Sunday afternoon.

Argentina and Spain are set to face off for the World Cup final at 3 p.m. ET in East Rutherford, New Jersey after a month of thrilling games that have gripped sports fans across the globe. The White House confirmed last week that Trump will be attending Sunday’s highly anticipated game as the U.S. caps off hosting one of the largest sporting events in the world.

“His attendance will cap what has been the most watched, most secure, and most successful World Cup in American history,” White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters. “This is a fitting conclusion to a tournament that showcased America’s ability to host the world on the grandest stage.”

FIFA president Gianni Infantino, a close ally of the president, also confirmed last month on “Fox & Friends” that he and Trump will jointly present the trophy to the winners of the World Cup.

“We will be together with the president enjoying the final and handing the trophy to the winner, of course, together,” Infantino said.

Trump faced criticism earlier this month after it was reported that he called Infantino asking FIFA to overturn the suspension of U.S. men’s national team striker Folarin Balogun. Balogun was ultimately allowed to play, but the U.S. men’s national team still lost to Belgium.

The appearance will mark the first time a U.S. president will attend the men’s World Cup final. The U.S. has hosted the tournament only once before, under then-President Bill Clinton.

When the U.S. last hosted the men’s World Cup in 1994, Clinton delivered remarks at the opening ceremony but did not attend the final match between Brazil and Italy. Instead, then-Vice President Al Gore attended the final game in Pasadena, California and handed the trophy to Brazil’s team captain.

But Clinton did attend the women’s final game in Pasadena when the U.S. women’s soccer team clinched their second World Cup title in 1999.
