title: Victory for football? Spain beat Argentina to win World Cup
description: It might seem like a trite cliche, but you won't find many neutrals disagreeing with that verdict on the World Cup final.
fullText:
Describing a result as a 'victory for football' might seem like a trite cliche - but you won't find many neutrals disagreeing with that description of Spain's win in the 2026 World Cup final.

When Ferran Torres slammed in the extra-time winner, and Rodri lifted the trophy high in New Jersey, it is hard to imagine many will have had sympathy for Argentina.

Spain triumphed in a battle of endurance, for players and spectators, to beat the Argentines and win their second men's World Cup.

On every football metric - and, some would say, morality too - this was the right result.

"There is no doubt that the right team won," former England captain Alan Shearer said on BBC One. "From start to finish, Spain controlled possession. They were the ones looking to play football, create chances and get in behind.

"This is a result for football because of the way that they have played. Spain deserve it."

Spain had 65% possession, an expected goals tally of 1.94 and 20 shots on goal - 11 on target.

At the other end, goalkeeper Unai Simon kept his seventh clean sheet in eight matches as he won the Golden Glove for the tournament's best goalkeeper.

Spain conceded just one goal at the 2026 World Cup - the fewest conceded by any winning team.

Spain are - even in the hard-to-quantify world of international football - clearly the preeminent side in the world.

They are world, Olympic and European champions - not to mention women's world champions too. The men are now unbeaten in their past 38 matches in all competitions - the longest run by any European team in history.

They stumbled out of the gates in this World Cup with a 0-0 draw against Cape Verde - meaning that on both occasions Spain have won the world title they have failed to win their opener, after a 1-0 loss to Switzerland in 2010.

There have only been three instances on record of a player completing 100 or more passes in a World Cup final. Two of those were Spain players on Sunday - Rodri with 101, and Pau Cubarsi with 121.

And for many of the squad, this might just be the start.

Cubarsi, 19, was named young player of the tournament, while it seems very likely his Barcelona team-mate Lamine Yamal has his best years ahead.

At 19 years and six days, Lamine Yamal became the third-youngest player to play in a World Cup final, after Pele in 1958 and Giuseppe Bergomi in 1982. He is now the third-youngest winner.

Sixteen titles in five years - why Spain are a dominant force

Lamine Yamal completes football at 19 as Argentina betray Messi

Trump, Bieber and Shakira - a World Cup final like never before

Argentina are so often painted as the pantomime villains of international football - particularly by England fans.

As they did in the semi-final against the Three Lions, they appeared in the first half to be more interested in stopping any semblance of football occurring than playing the game themselves.

Leandro Paredes was the spoiler in chief, with Enzo Fernandez his deputy.

Though four Argentina players were booked, many will feel that adds further to the theory they have had lenient treatment from officials at this tournament.

Argentina made a total of 464 passes, compared to Spain's 852.

But, unlike England, Spain did not wilt in the second half. Had they won this game long before Torres' 106th-minute strike there could have been few complaints.

Argentina's Emiliano Martinez had to make 11 saves - the most by any goalkeeper on record in a World Cup final.

At the other end, meanwhile, the defending champions became the first team in World Cup history not to have a shot in 90 minutes of a final. Their first attempt came via Lionel Messi in the 117th minute.

Argentina lost their crown, and the scenes that followed the final whistle led many to feel they had lost their dignity too.

Fernandez was first shown a yellow card for dissent, then a second for a spectacularly late challenge that sent Cubarsi somersaulting.

After the full-time whistle, Paredes was involved in scraps with Rodri, Gavi and Borja Iglesias and team mate Nahuel Molina was seen throwing a punch at a passing Spain player too.

"Embarrassing, seeing that sort of stuff after the game has been played," said BBC pundit Wayne Rooney. "Someone has been crowned World Cup champions. It is not good enough.

"Paredes is better than that. It doesn't stop there. He keeps going and going."

Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni attempted to defend his side's performance through floods of tears at his post-match media conference, but could only admit the best team had won.

"Even though they were out-playing us until the 85th minute, I would love to know what would have happened had we still had 11 men on the pitch," he said of Fernandez's dismissal.

"We managed to go all the way to the end when the match was so open and it just couldn't be. They deserved the victory, that is the reality."

Argentina might have taken the World Cup final to extra time - the fifth of the past six to go that far - but that could not mask their inferiority.

"Argentina can have absolutely no complaints about what happened," former England goalkeeper Joe Hart told BBC One. "Disgusting behaviour.

"I don't think Rodri needed to get in their faces but you understand that after 120 minutes where you have been wound up and you thought you've been wronged, and the referee has been on your back."

title: ‘They’d call us animals’ – World Cup final security slammed amid two-hour queues and ‘total chaos’
description: The security outside of the New York New Jersey Stadium ahead of the 2026 World Cup final has been criticized. Supporters attending the match have faced long queues just to enter the stadium before Spain play Argentina at 3pm EDT.
fullText:
The security outside of the New York New Jersey Stadium ahead of the 2026 World Cup final has been criticized.

Supporters attending the match have faced long queues just to enter the stadium before Spain play Argentina at 3pm EDT.

The United States Secret Service are said to have taken over security measures for the game because US president Donald Trump will be attending a match for the first time during this summer’s tournament.

Just a few hours before kick-off, fans and journalists launched fierce criticisms of the operation.

Mariana Spinelli, who works for leading Brazilian outlet Globo, took to social media platform X to write: “We’ve been in line for 2 hours for the x-ray to ENTER the stadium. Everything disorganized, a total chaos. If this were in South America, they’d call us animals, third-world country. Since it’s in the US nobody says anything. A nightmare of organization, pathetic.”

Spinelli is clearly of the belief that if such events were to happen in South America, there would be a much higher level of outrage.

Zach Lowy, the chief editor of Breaking The Lines, wrote: “The organization for this World Cup Final has been shambolic. Thousands who’ve been waiting for 3 hours, there’s a lack of security and officials and scanners, a lot of people who are cutting in line, and a lot of people who have gone around in circles without any progress.”

A further complication also arose when soccer fans were warned to attend the stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey up to four hours before the kick off time. Gates opened at 11am EDT.

A glitch in the FIFA security system to scan passes resulted in long queues forming.

There are believed to be two levels of security at the stadium, one of which is run by the Secret Service and the other organized by the Transportation Security Administration (TSA).

TSA usually oversee airport security. Fans and members of the media attending Sunday’s World Cup final having to pass through airport style scanners before being admitted into the venue.

title: When will the United States host the World Cup again?
description: Fans likely won't have to wait too long to see the World Cup return to the U.S.
fullText:
The United States had to wait 32 years between opportunities to host the men's World Cup. But after the final whistle sounds in Sunday's World Cup Final between Spain and Argentina at MetLife Stadium, U.S. fans shouldn't expect nearly as long of a gap between World Cups stateside.

FIFA's questionable manipulation of the hosting cycles will make that possible.

Basically, FIFA's process for choosing the World Cup hosts goes in a cycle between continental confederations. The 2030 World Cup wasn't just awarded to Spain and Portugal, but Morocco also joined the bid to use Africa's turn in the cycle with Europe. Then, FIFA announced that it would hold opening centennial matches in Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay. This exhausted CONMEBOL's (South America) turn in the cycle.

While this maneuvering was an obvious effort to award the 2034 World Cup to Saudi Arabia ahead of its usual turn, it also made a quicker return to the U.S. a near certainty.

FIFA has yet to award the 2038 World Cup hosting rights, but fans should expect the tournament to return to the U.S., Mexico and Canada.

Only Oceania and North America would be eligible to host the 2038 World Cup under the current cycle rules. But with Australia competing in the Asian confederation, no viable host exists in Oceania. New Zealand doesn't have the stadium infrastructure.

And given the success of the 2026 World Cup, FIFA is absolutely going to look to cash in on another U.S. World Cup as soon as possible.

Countries eligible to host the 2038 FIFA World Cup 

(by u/-yolewpaniaq) pic.twitter.com/dOEAsoAHAN

— OnlMaps (@onlmaps) July 18, 2026

The 2031 Women's World Cup will also be in North America with Mexico, the United States, Costa Rica and Jamaica hosting the tournament.

This article originally appeared on For The Win: When will the United States host the World Cup again?

title: IShowSpeed kicks off World Cup closing ceremony in blaze of colour
description: Another star-studded performance at half-time will include Shakira, BTS, Madonna and Justin Bieber.
fullText:
The 2026 World Cup closing ceremony has kicked off with a performance from streamer IShowSpeed, who took to the field alongside drummers and dancers in vibrant tracksuits.

Rapper and singer Post Malone followed, performing his hit song Wow.

More than 80,000 fans, celebrities and foreign dignitaries are expected to pack New York New Jersey Stadium for Sunday's final between Spain and Argentina.

The star-studded pre-match entertainment is also expected to bring in actor Tom Cruise alongside singers Robbie Williams, Laura Pausini and Nicole Scherzinger.

Watch closing ceremony and build-up before Spain face Argentina in World Cup final

Italian singer Pausini told the BBC's Carl Nasman that she's "very excited" and "proud" to be performing alongside Williams and Scherzinger at the closing ceremony.

"When Robbie [Williams] invited me to sing with him, I felt goosebumps everywhere because Robbie's one of my favorite singer, songwriter, and performer," Pausini told the BBC.

The celebrity line-up will continue at half-time with performances by Madonna, Justin Bieber, Shakira and BTS.

Madonna, the best-selling female music artist of all time, is set to perform after the launch of her new album, Confessions II, which debuted at number one on the Billboard 200 chart in July.

Bieber also returned to the spotlight earlier this year, with his biggest live performance in four years at the Coachella music festival after cancelling his Justice world tour because of health issues.

Colombian pop star Shakira and Nigerian musician Burna Boy will reunite on stage after performing together at the tournament's opening ceremony in Mexico City last month.

Their collaboration, Dai Dai, has been a global chart hit and has been a fixture in stadiums throughout the tournament.

K-Pop group BTS will also perform after making their comeback from a three-year music hiatus to complete their mandatory military service in South Korea. The band is currently in the midst of an 85-date world tour.

The half-time break is expected to last 20-25 minutes, with Fifa saying the Super-Bowl-style performance will take 11 minutes.

It has been curated by Coldplay's Chris Martin and will raise money for the Fifa Global Citizen Education Fund, an initiative working to raise $100m (£73m) for children worldwide.

title: Donald Trump goes off-script on 'bagpipes' tangent in World Cup Final interview
description: President Donald Trump was interviewed ahead of the FIFA World Cup Final between Argentina and Spain, but the 80-year-old went off-script and began rambling about Scotland and bagpipes during the conversation
fullText:
President Donald Trump went off on a tangent during a pre-match interview ahead of the FIFA World Cup Final.

On July 19, Argentina faces Spain in the Final of the World Cup. The two nations battle for the world championship title at MetLife Stadium, branded as New York/New Jersey Stadium by the tournament, in East Rutherford, New Jersey.

Prior to the match on Fox Sports Live, President Donald Trump, 80, was questioned about how the United States Men's National Team had captured the world's attention, via The Mirror US.

Donald Trump sparks outrage with Harry Kane World Cup comments

Kylian Mbappe drops World Cup truth bomb after absurd third-place loss to England

Instead, Trump veered off-topic and began talking about Scotland — this following his boasts about pressuring FIFA chief Gianni Infantino over USMNT striker Folarin Balogun's red card, which triggered widespread controversy.

TV personality Jenny Taft, 38, posed the question: "It has been so special to see fans all over the world fall in love with our country. How proud does that make you?"

Trump responded: "Well, I watched, and it started with Scotland, and all of a sudden they're doing things that nobody has ever seen with the bagpipes. And it was beautiful.

"And it just went to every country. And we're the hottest country in the world right now," he concluded.

Trump's previous World Cup comments also drew significant backlash. England head coach Thomas Tuchel delivered a sharp rebuke after the President took aim at the team following their World Cup semifinal defeat to Argentina, during which Trump specifically criticized the tactical deployment of captain Harry Kane.

To listen to the latest episode of 'All Out Soccer' - CLICK HERE

"Harry, who's been fantastic, I think they perhaps made a mistake when they made him a defensive player. What do I know about soccer?" Trump initially said.

"They took the lead, and they took their best player and then put him on defense. What do I know about coaching? But that was a little unusual," he closed.

Tuchel was questioned about Trump's remarks ahead of England's third-place match against France on July 18. "Do you use Donald Trump as your witness for the case?" the ex-Chelsea boss responded to the reporter.

England then produced their finest performance of the tournament. England defeated France 6-4 in a thriller.

Following the victory over the French, Tuchel dismissed Trump's criticism and declared, "This game will help us for sure – even if you can never fully celebrate a bronze medal.

"It's the first World Cup medal for 60 years, first at a World Cup on foreign soil, and I hope the players can be proud of that at some point.

"We set ourselves the highest dream to chase, and we were very, very ambitious with our dream to make the final in the World Cup. So it's very, very painful if you miss out; the pain will stay for a while," he finished.

title: Trump arrives at World Cup final for Argentina vs. Spain matchup
description: Trump will watch final as he demands U.S. host World Cup again ‘immediately’
fullText:
Donald Trump arrived at the final of the World Cup on Sunday afternoon, where the U.S. president will take in his first and only match of the championship as Argentina and Spain battle it out for the trophy.

Pool reports indicated that Trump departed Bedminster, New Jersey via Marine One just before 1:00 p.m. eastern time, and reporters were quickly afterwards ushered onto the field for the pre-game events. His helicopter completed a brief flyover, which went largely unnoticed by the crowd, according to reporters in the stadium. Marine One then touched down outside the arena.

The president hosted a press conference on Friday as he hosted a reception with FIFA’s president, Gianni Infantino, and said that he’d bring up the idea of hosting the championship again to Infantino. Hosting rights for the next two World Cups have already been decided.
